reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Comprehensive event type table enumerates all 17 event types from the source type union — gives the reader a definitive reference without needing to guess.
The 'Avoiding infinite loops' section is exactly the kind of production gotcha a first-time user will hit; it's concrete, actionable, and covers three distinct prevention strategies.
URL-encoding warning on object keys in the event payload is a genuine, easy-to-miss bug — surfaced early and with a concrete fix.

FAQ section covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, AWS service mechanics (delivery guarantees, retry behavior, notification latency), and choosing/comparison questions (Lambda vs batch job). 9 quality entries with no padding.
Handler code example in a fenced TypeScript block (application code, not config) is realistic and copyable — correctly separated from the CodeBlock intellisense config examples.
Clear 'When NOT to use' H2 with specific alternatives (SQS for batching, batch job for long-running work, SNS/EventBridge for fan-out) rather than vague guidance.
Both Lambda and batch job trigger targets are covered with dedicated working examples.
problems: Missing `<ApiReference>` component — the default page rhythm (item 7) requires exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="..." />` per page. The source type is `S3IntegrationProps`, which covers `bucketArn`, `s3EventType`, and `filterRule`. Its absence means the reader has no structured drill-down for property discovery.

'Why use S3 triggers' is an H2 heading in question-initiating form ('Why…'). The style guide requires noun-form H2 labels (1–5 words, e.g., 'Overview', 'Use cases'). The equivalent structured content lives naturally as the intro paragraph rather than a separate H2.

`cors: { enabled: true }` on the `Bucket` in the basic example is unrelated to S3 trigger configuration and introduces an unexplained property that the reader may think is required for triggers to work.
mandatoryFixes: Add `<ApiReference definitionName="S3IntegrationProps" />` to the page (bottom is the conventional placement for tutorial-style pages). The default page rhythm requires exactly one per page — it is currently absent entirely.
optionalImprovements: Remove `cors: { enabled: true }` from the basic example Bucket — it is unrelated to trigger wiring and silently implies CORS is needed for S3 triggers.
Rename 'Why use S3 triggers' to a compact noun-form label such as 'Overview' or fold the paragraph into the opening section, eliminating the extra H2 that breaks the noun-form heading convention.

In the paragraph describing `connectTo`, add a one-sentence note that connecting to a bucket also injects bucket connection details (ARN, name) as environment variables into the Lambda, using the `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_*` naming pattern — currently the prose only mentions IAM permissions.

The basic example uses `imagesBucket.arn` as the `bucketArn` value but the surrounding prose explains this only one paragraph later. Move the explanation of `.arn` resolution (or a brief parenthetical) into the code comment zone or directly above the example so readers aren't left wondering on first read.

Consider adding a brief callout or note that the S3 notification configuration supports only one Lambda destination per event type + filter combination per bucket — this constraint is mentioned in the 'When NOT to use' section and the FAQ, but a prominent callout near the filtering section would help readers who skim past the top.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Complete and accurate event-type table covering all 17 S3 event types verbatim from the source, with plain-English descriptions of each.
'Avoiding infinite loops' section is genuinely production-useful — explains the failure mode, names concrete prevention strategies, and pairs it with a <Tip> that reinforces the advice.
URL-encoding warning on object keys is a real gotcha that trips up most first-time implementers; flagging it with a <Warning> and showing the decode pattern is the right call.
Retry-behavior section correctly describes S3's asynchronous invocation model (at-least-once, three total attempts) and gives actionable idempotency guidance.
FAQ mixes all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, AWS-general, and choosing/comparison — and answers are concise enough to work as featured snippets.
CodeBlock usage is correct throughout: TypeScript class-based syntax with defineConfig, proper focus markers, no hand-authored YAML tabs.
Filter-rule section is clear and well-scoped, with a working example that shows prefix+suffix together and a prose explanation of the AND-semantics.
problems: Missing <ApiReference definitionName="S3IntegrationProps" /> entirely. The backbone requires exactly one ApiReference on every trigger/resource page, and this page has none. Readers cannot drill into the full property list without navigating elsewhere.

Overly long H2 headings violate the 1-5 word noun-form label rule: 'Why use S3 triggers' (question-form, 4 words but reads like a sentence) and 'When NOT to use S3 triggers' (6 words, clearly a sentence). Shorter labels work better here.

The claim that S3 events can trigger batch jobs is not grounded in the provided source. The S3Integration JSDoc says 'Triggers a function when files are created, deleted, or restored in an S3 bucket' — 'function' only, no mention of batch jobs. Without BatchJob source confirming S3Integration is accepted in its events array, this is an unverified claim.

The basic example includes `cors: { enabled: true }` on the Bucket with no explanation. CORS controls browser access, not Lambda trigger behavior. Including it in a trigger-focused example suggests it is required or relevant to triggering, which it is not. It adds noise without documentation.

The FAQ answer about external buckets says 'this may require manual bucket policy adjustments on the external bucket.' The actual requirement is configuring the bucket's notification configuration (event notifications), not its bucket policy. These are different AWS constructs; the wording is inaccurate.
mandatoryFixes: Add <ApiReference definitionName="S3IntegrationProps" /> to the page — place it at the bottom after the FAQ, or wherever fits the flow. The backbone requires exactly one ApiReference on every trigger page.

Shorten H2 headings to compact noun-form labels: change 'Why use S3 triggers' to 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use S3 triggers' to 'When not to use'. Both currently read as sentences; the style guide mandates 1-5 word noun-form labels (e.g., 'Scaling', 'Networking', 'When to use').

Either remove the 'Using with batch jobs' section and all batch-job references in the intro/FAQ, or ground the claim in the BatchJob type source showing that S3Integration is accepted in a batch job's events array. The S3Integration JSDoc in the provided source says 'Triggers a function' — not a batch job. Publishing an unverified claim that S3 events can trigger batch jobs will mislead users who try it and encounter a type error or runtime failure.
optionalImprovements: Remove `cors: { enabled: true }` from the Bucket in the basic example. It has nothing to do with S3 event triggers and will confuse readers into thinking it is required.

Correct the FAQ answer about external buckets: replace 'bucket policy adjustments' with 'bucket event notification configuration'. The external bucket needs a notification configuration pointing to the Lambda ARN, not a bucket policy change. These are different AWS constructs.

The 'Throughput and retry behavior' section states that the dead-letter queue must be configured 'on the Lambda function'. Consider noting that Stacktape exposes this via the Lambda function's `onFailure` destination property (if source-grounded) so readers know where to configure it without leaving the page.

The FAQ answer 'Can I filter S3 events by object size or content type?' could note that AWS EventBridge S3 notifications (a separate AWS feature, not covered here) do support richer metadata filtering — this gives readers a forward path if they need it, without inflating the page.

Consider adding a one-sentence note near the `bucketArn` field explanation that covers the ARN form expected (e.g., `arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket`) for readers who use external buckets and are unsure of the format.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.

verifierResults.summary: The S3 Events draft is well-structured and mostly accurate. issues.statement: Stacktape supports the full set of S3 event notification types.
issues.reason: The source file defines a specific union type for s3EventType that does NOT include all AWS S3 event notification types. AWS supports additional event types such as s3:ObjectTagging:*, s3:ObjectTagging:Put, s3:ObjectTagging:Delete, s3:LifecycleExpiration:*, s3:LifecycleTransition, s3:IntelligentTiering, and s3:ObjectAcl:Put — none of which appear in the source union. Claiming 'the full set' is a Stacktape-specific overstatement that would mislead a reader into trying an unsupported event type.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace 'Stacktape supports the full set of S3 event notification types' with 'The s3EventType property determines which bucket operations trigger the function. Stacktape supports the following S3 event notification types:' — letting the table speak for itself without overclaiming completeness.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

evidence.quote:
interface S3IntegrationProps {
  /**
   * #### The ARN of the S3 bucket to monitor for events.
   */
  bucketArn: string;
  /**
   * #### The type of S3 event that will trigger the function.
   */
  s3EventType:
    | 's3:ReducedRedundancyLostObject'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:*'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Put'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Copy'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:CompleteMultipartUpload'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:Delete'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:DeleteMarkerCreated'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Completed'
    | 's3:Replication:*'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationFailedReplication'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationNotTracked'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationMissedThreshold'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationReplicatedAfterThreshold';
  /**
   * #### A filter to apply to objects, so the function is only triggered for relevant objects.
   */
  filterRule?: S3FilterRule;
}

issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the source: “S3Integration takes a bucketArn string. For a Stacktape Bucket resource, pass bucket.arn; for an existing bucket, pass its ARN if your stack is allowed to configure notifications for that bucket.”

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

It does not document S3Integration dead-letter behavior, Lambda async failure destinations, or that failed events are always discarded. The generated-doc source is more cautious and says retry behavior and dead-letter queue settings are part of Lambda function configuration, and events are discarded unless a dead-letter queue is configured. Under Lambda asynchronous invocation behavior, AWS retries failed async invocations; if no failure destination or dead-letter queue is configured, failed events are not retained for later processing.”

evidence.quote:
interface S3IntegrationProps {
  /**
   * #### The ARN of the S3 bucket to monitor for events.
   */
  bucketArn: string;
  /**
   * #### The type of S3 event that will trigger the function.
   */
  s3EventType:
    | 's3:ReducedRedundancyLostObject'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:*'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Put'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:Copy'
    | 's3:ObjectCreated:CompleteMultipartUpload'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:Delete'
    | 's3:ObjectRemoved:DeleteMarkerCreated'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:*'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Post'
    | 's3:ObjectRestore:Completed'
    | 's3:Replication:*'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationFailedReplication'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationNotTracked'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationMissedThreshold'
    | 's3:Replication:OperationReplicatedAfterThreshold';
  /**
   * #### A filter to apply to objects, so the function is only triggered for relevant objects.
   */
  filterRule?: S3FilterRule;
}
Retry behavior and dead-letter queue settings are part of the [Lambda function](/resources/compute/lambda-function) configuration.
If all attempts fail, the event is discarded unless you have configured a dead-letter queue on the Lambda function to capture failed events for later investigation.
